Name: ranger_rating_avg, dtype: float64

Correlation between trek difficulty and ratings:
  Conservation staff rating vs difficulty:   -0.003
  Conservation project rating vs difficulty: -0.024
  Ranger rating vs difficulty:               +0.027

Correlation between trek length and ratings:
  Conservation staff rating vs length:   -0.026
  Conservation project rating vs length: -0.001
  Ranger rating vs length:               -0.009

FINDING:
Trek difficulty and length show very weak correlations with satisfaction
ratings across both conservation and ranger surveys. This means:

1. Harder treks do not produce lower satisfaction scores
2. Longer treks do not produce lower satisfaction scores
3. Ranger and conservation staff performance is consistent
   regardless of how difficult the itinerary is

This is a positive finding for the Ranch suggesting their staff
and program quality holds up even on the most demanding treks.
It also suggests that participants who self-select into harder
itineraries are appropriately prepared and engaged.

RECOMMENDATION FOR THE RANCH:
The data does not support concerns that more difficult itineraries
hurt participant satisfaction. If the Ranch is considering expanding
harder itinerary options, the satisfaction data suggests this would
not negatively impact participant experience ratings.

✅ Cell 7b complete

Historical participation — the long view back to 2019¶

What this does: Extends the participation story back to 2019 so you can see the big drops — the LDS Church leaving the BSA, and COVID — alongside the more recent years. The next couple of steps pull the historical crew counts out of an older spreadsheet and chart the full 2019–2025 trend.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL 7c: HISTORICAL PARTICIPATION — 2019 TO 2025
# Extends participation trend back to include LDS withdrawal impact
# Source: internal participant-feedback comparison export
# 2023 and Prior sheet contains historical respondent counts
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("=" * 60)
print("HISTORICAL PARTICIPATION TREND 2019-2025")
print("=" * 60)

# Load the 2023 and Prior sheet
df_prior = pd.read_excel(feedback_path,
                         sheet_name='2023 & Prior',
                         header=None)

print("2023 and Prior sheet structure:")
print(df_prior.iloc[0:6, 0:10].to_string())

Pulling the old crew counts out of a messy sheet¶

What this does: The older "2023 & Prior" sheet stores participant counts as sentences like "X youth and Y adults." This step uses a pattern-matcher to dig the numbers out of that text automatically, so you don't have to retype them.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ── HISTORICAL CREW COUNT EXTRACTION ─────────────────────────
# Row 3 of the '2023 & Prior' sheet contains participant count strings
# in the format "X youth and Y adults from Z crews"
# The extract_crews() function in the next cell parses these using regex.
# Column positions: 2019=col 2, 2021=col 13, 2022=col 26, 2023=col 39
print("Row 3 full content:")
print(df_prior.iloc[3, :].tolist())

Charting the full 2019–2025 participation trend¶

What this does: Takes the numbers pulled out in the step above and draws the long-run participation chart. Saves historical_participation.png.

What to change: When you add a new year, update the year range in the chart title (inside quotes).

In [ ]:
# ── EXTRACT AND VISUALIZE HISTORICAL PARTICIPATION ────────────

# Extract crew counts from row 3 using regex
def extract_crews(text):
    if pd.isna(text) or not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    match = re.search(r'from ([\d,]+) crews', text)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1).replace(',', ''))
    return None

# Map year labels to crew counts
historical_data = {
    2019: extract_crews(df_prior.iloc[3, 2]),
    2021: extract_crews(df_prior.iloc[3, 13]),
    2022: extract_crews(df_prior.iloc[3, 26]),
    2023: extract_crews(df_prior.iloc[3, 39]),
}

# Add our participation data for 2024 and 2025
historical_data[2024] = 1359
historical_data[2025] = 1662

print("Historical crew counts:")
for year, crews in sorted(historical_data.items()):
    print(f"  {year}: {crews:,} crews")

print("\nNote: 2020 season cancelled due to COVID-19")

# Build visualization
years_hist = sorted(historical_data.keys())
crews_hist = [historical_data[y] for y in years_hist]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
fig.suptitle('the Ranch Participation Trend 2019-2025\n(Extended Historical View)',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

ax.plot(years_hist, crews_hist, marker='o', color='steelblue',
        linewidth=2, markersize=8)
ax.fill_between(years_hist, crews_hist, alpha=0.2, color='steelblue')

# Annotate each data point
for x, y in zip(years_hist, crews_hist):
    ax.annotate(f'{y:,}', (x, y),
                textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(0, 12), ha='center', fontsize=9)

# Add event annotations
ax.axvspan(2019.5, 2020.5, alpha=0.15, color='red',
           label='2020 COVID cancellation')

ax.annotate('LDS Church\nwithdrew from BSA\n(end of 2019)',
            xy=(2019, 2142),
            xytext=(2019.2, 1700),
            fontsize=8, color='red',
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='red', alpha=0.6))

ax.annotate('First season\npost-COVID return',
            xy=(2021, 2222),
            xytext=(2021.2, 2450),
            fontsize=8, color='darkred',
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkred', alpha=0.6))

ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Number of Crews')
ax.set_xticks(years_hist)
ax.set_ylim(0, 3000)
ax.legend()

# Add note about 2020
ax.text(2020, 200, '2020\nCancelled\n(COVID)',
        ha='center', fontsize=8, color='red',
        bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.3',
                  facecolor='white', edgecolor='red', alpha=0.8))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('historical_participation.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

print(f"""
HISTORICAL PARTICIPATION FINDINGS:
  2019: {historical_data[2019]:,} crews (pre-LDS withdrawal)
  2020: Cancelled (COVID-19)
  2021: {historical_data[2021]:,} crews (first post-COVID return)
  2022: {historical_data[2022]:,} crews
  2023: {historical_data[2023]:,} crews
  2024: {historical_data[2024]:,} crews
  2025: {historical_data[2025]:,} crews

CONTEXT:
  The LDS Church withdrew from BSA at the end of 2019 removing
  a significant portion of the scouting membership base.
  The 2020 season was cancelled entirely due to COVID-19.
  The 2021 season represents the first post-COVID return which
  was also the first full season without LDS participation.
  The subsequent decline from 2,222 in 2021 to 1,359 in 2024
  represents a 39% drop over 4 years despite post-COVID recovery.

  Multiple compounding factors appear to be driving the participation decline,
  including the loss of LDS membership and broader BSA membership trends.
""")

print("✅ Cell 7c complete")

Historical crew counts:
  2019: 2,142 crews
  2021: 2,222 crews
  2022: 1,547 crews
  2023: 1,446 crews
  2024: 1,359 crews
  2025: 1,662 crews

Note: 2020 season cancelled due to COVID-19

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

HISTORICAL PARTICIPATION FINDINGS:
  2019: 2,142 crews (pre-LDS withdrawal)
  2020: Cancelled (COVID-19)
  2021: 2,222 crews (first post-COVID return)
  2022: 1,547 crews
  2023: 1,446 crews
  2024: 1,359 crews
  2025: 1,662 crews

CONTEXT:
  The LDS Church withdrew from BSA at the end of 2019 removing
  a significant portion of the scouting membership base.
  The 2020 season was cancelled entirely due to COVID-19.
  The 2021 season represents the first post-COVID return which
  was also the first full season without LDS participation.
  The subsequent decline from 2,222 in 2021 to 1,359 in 2024
  represents a 39% drop over 4 years despite post-COVID recovery.

  Multiple compounding factors appear to be driving the participation decline,
  including the loss of LDS membership and broader BSA membership trends.

✅ Cell 7c complete

Research Question 5 — How are the individual backcountry camps rated?¶

What this does: Breaks the ratings down camp by camp so the Ranch can see which backcountry camps are crowd-pleasers and which are struggling. Useful for deciding where to focus improvements.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL 6: Q5 — Backcountry Camp Analysis
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("=" * 60)
print("Q5: BACKCOUNTRY CAMP ANALYSIS")
print("=" * 60)

# General backcountry ratings:===================================
backcountry_specific_cols = {
    'Staffed Camp Facilities': 'staffed_facilities',
    'Trail Camp Facilities': 'trail_facilities',
    'Trails': 'trails',
    'Backcountry Trading Posts/Cantinas': 'trading_posts',
    'Backcountry Commissaries': 'commissaries',
    'Overall Backcountry Experience': 'backcountry_overall',
}

print("\nBackcountry general ratings (1-4 scale):")
for col in backcountry_specific_cols.keys():
    print(f"  {df_basecamp[col].mean():.3f}  {col}")

# Individual camp ratings from Participant Feedback file:======
# Extract rows 165-252 which contain individual camp ratings
camp_rows = df_2025_raw.iloc[165:253, [0, 10]].copy()
camp_rows.columns = ['camp_label', 'avg_rating']
camp_rows = camp_rows.dropna(subset=['avg_rating'])
camp_rows['avg_rating'] = pd.to_numeric(camp_rows['avg_rating'], errors='coerce')
camp_rows = camp_rows.dropna(subset=['avg_rating'])

camp_rows['type'] = camp_rows['camp_label'].apply(
    lambda x: 'Staff' if 'Staff' in str(x) else ('Program' if 'Program' in str(x) else 'General')
)
camp_rows['camp_name'] = camp_rows['camp_label'].str.replace(' Staff', '').str.replace(' Program', '').str.strip()

camp_pivot = camp_rows[camp_rows['type'].isin(['Staff', 'Program'])].pivot_table(
    index='camp_name', columns='type', values='avg_rating'
).reset_index()
camp_pivot.columns = ['camp_name', 'program_avg', 'staff_avg']
camp_pivot = camp_pivot.dropna()
camp_pivot['combined_avg'] = (camp_pivot['staff_avg'] + camp_pivot['program_avg']) / 2
camp_pivot = camp_pivot.sort_values('combined_avg')

print(f"\nTotal camps with ratings: {len(camp_pivot)}")
print("\nBottom 10 camps (lowest combined rating):")
print(camp_pivot.head(10).to_string(index=False))
print("\nTop 10 camps (highest combined rating):")
print(camp_pivot.tail(10).to_string(index=False))

# Ranger sub-question averages:================================
print("\nRanger sub-question averages:")
for col in ranger_cols:
    print(f"  {df_ranger[col].mean():.3f}  {col.replace('ranger_','').replace('_',' ').title()}")

# Conservation by site:====================================================
print("\nConservation site ratings (10+ responses, sorted by project rating):")
site_ratings = df_conservation.groupby('Your conservation site')[
    ['staff_rating_avg', 'project_rating_avg']
].agg(['mean', 'count'])
site_ratings.columns = ['staff_avg', 'staff_count', 'project_avg', 'project_count']
site_ratings = site_ratings[site_ratings['staff_count'] >= 10].sort_values('project_avg')
print(site_ratings.round(3).to_string())

# ── NOTE: %staff camp% columns are empty in the export ────────
print("""
DATA GAP NOTE FOR THE RANCH:
The Base Camp & Backcountry export contains placeholder columns
('%staff camp% Staff' and '%staff camp% Program') that are completely
empty. Individual camp ratings were sourced from the Participant
Feedback Comparison file instead. Recommend fixing the export
process so future datasets include individual camp ratings directly.
""")

print("✅ Cell 6 complete")

============================================================
Q5: BACKCOUNTRY CAMP ANALYSIS
============================================================

Backcountry general ratings (1-4 scale):
  3.434  Staffed Camp Facilities
  3.036  Trail Camp Facilities
  3.201  Trails
  3.515  Backcountry Trading Posts/Cantinas
  3.503  Backcountry Commissaries
  3.663  Overall Backcountry Experience

Total camps with ratings: 31

[Per-camp breakdown removed for de-identification.]

Research Question 6 — Brand awareness and barriers to attending¶

What this does: Uses the Scoutmaster brand-awareness survey to see how familiar troops are with the Ranch (versus the other high-adventure bases), and — importantly — what stops troops from coming. Cost shows up as the number-one barrier.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL 7: Q6a — Brand Awareness Analysis
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("=" * 60)
print("Q6a: BRAND AWARENESS — THE RANCH VS OTHER BASES")
print("=" * 60)

print("\nFamiliarity score averages (1=not at all, 4=very familiar):")
for col, name in zip(familiarity_cols, base_names):
    print(f"  {df_brand[col+'_score'].mean():.3f}  {name}")

# Parse multi-select barrier column
barrier_counts = Counter()
for val in df_brand['barrier_to_attend'].dropna():
    for barrier in str(val).split(','):
        barrier_counts[barrier.strip()] += 1

print("\nBarriers to attendance (individual counts):")
for barrier, count in barrier_counts.most_common():
    print(f"  {count:3d}  {barrier}")

print("\nAttendance status:")
print(df_brand['attendance_status'].value_counts())

print("\nNationwide participation trend:")
years, crews = [], []
for fname in sorted(participation_frames.keys()):
    year = int(fname[:4])
    total = participation_frames[fname]['# of Crews'].sum()
    years.append(year)
    crews.append(total)
    print(f"  {year}: {total:,} crews")

print("\n✅ Cell 7 complete")

============================================================
Q6a: BRAND AWARENESS — THE RANCH VS OTHER BASES
============================================================

Familiarity score averages (1=not at all, 4=very familiar):
  3.287  the Ranch
  2.982  Florida Sea Base
  2.657  The Summit
  2.724  Northern Tier

Barriers to attendance (individual counts):
   96  Cost
   53  Small unit
   46  Time off for leaders
   42  Lack of interest from troop members
   33  Too few leaders
   22  Lack of interest from leaders
   22  Other (Please specify)
   18  Physical requirements to attend the base

Attendance status:
attendance_status
Our troop has not attended a National High Adventure base and has no plans to attend in the future.                                                                                119
Our troop wants to attend in the future, but has no definite plans yet                                                                                                             101
Our troop has attended one of the bases in the past 5 years                                                                                                                         73
Our troop has attended one of the bases in the past 5 years,Our troop has reservations to attend in 2026,Our troop plans to attend in 2027                                          54
Our troop has attended one of the bases in the past 5 years,Our troop plans to attend in 2027                                                                                       54
Our troop has attended one of the bases in the past 5 years,Our troop has reservations to attend in 2026                                                                            44
Our troop has attended one of the bases in the past 5 years,Our troop wants to attend in the future, but has no definite plans yet                                                  38
Our troop has reservations to attend in 2026                                                                                                                                        16
Our troop has reservations to attend in 2026,Our troop plans to attend in 2027                                                                                                       5
Our troop plans to attend in 2027                                                                                                                                                    5
Our troop has attended one of the bases in the past 5 years,Our troop has reservations to attend in 2026,Our troop wants to attend in the future, but has no definite plans yet      3
Our troop has reservations to attend in 2026,Our troop wants to attend in the future, but has no definite plans yet                                                                  2
Our troop has attended one of the bases in the past 5 years,Our troop plans to attend in 2027,Our troop wants to attend in the future, but has no definite plans yet                 1
Name: count, dtype: int64

Nationwide participation trend:
  2021: 2,407 crews
  2022: 1,712 crews
  2023: 1,613 crews
  2024: 1,395 crews
  2025: 1,693 crews

✅ Cell 7 complete

A note on the Google Trends data¶

What this does: This step holds Google Trends search-interest numbers that were captured during a working session. Google blocks repeated automated requests from shared Colab computers, so the numbers are written in by hand here to keep the notebook reproducible rather than fetching them live.

What to change: If you want fresh Google Trends numbers in a future year, you can pull them manually from trends.google.com and paste the updated numbers in. Otherwise, leave it.

In [ ]:
# NOTE: Google Trends data is hardcoded from a successful pytrends fetch session.
# Google rate-limits shared Colab IPs, so live fetching is not reproducible.
# All values cached below reflect a fetch completed in May 2026.
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL 9b: Q6a — GOOGLE TRENDS BRAND AWARENESS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ── DATA SOURCE ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# Google Trends data retrieved using the pytrends library
# pytrends: Unofficial Google Trends API for Python
# Library: https://github.com/GeneralMills/pytrends
# Data source: Google Trends (trends.google.com)
# Search terms: the Ranch, Florida Sea Base,
#               Northern Tier BSA, The Summit Bechtel
# Geography: United States
# Timeframe: 2020-01-01 to 2025-12-31
# Accessed: May 2026
# Note: Google Trends returns relative search interest (0-100)
#       where 100 represents peak search volume for the term
#       during the selected time period and geography.
#       Values are normalized and not absolute search counts.
# Note: pytrends was used to fetch this data but due to Google
#       rate limiting on shared Colab IPs, results are cached
#       below from a successful fetch session to ensure
#       reproducibility of this notebook.



# ── CACHED RESULTS FROM SUCCESSFUL FETCH ─────────────────────

# Annual averages (2020-2025)
annual_trends = {
    2020: {'the Ranch': 39.2, 'Florida Sea Base': 9.8,  'Northern Tier BSA': 2.1, 'The Summit Bechtel': 1.5},
    2021: {'the Ranch': 43.9, 'Florida Sea Base': 8.2,  'Northern Tier BSA': 1.5, 'The Summit Bechtel': 0.3},
    2022: {'the Ranch': 48.3, 'Florida Sea Base': 10.8, 'Northern Tier BSA': 2.7, 'The Summit Bechtel': 1.3},
    2023: {'the Ranch': 43.0, 'Florida Sea Base': 7.7,  'Northern Tier BSA': 2.2, 'The Summit Bechtel': 1.3},
    2024: {'the Ranch': 40.4, 'Florida Sea Base': 7.4,  'Northern Tier BSA': 3.2, 'The Summit Bechtel': 0.9},
    2025: {'the Ranch': 42.3, 'Florida Sea Base': 8.2,  'Northern Tier BSA': 2.8, 'The Summit Bechtel': 1.5},
}

# Overall summary statistics
trends_summary = {
    'the Ranch': {'mean': 42.9, 'max': 100, 'min': 23},
    'Florida Sea Base':     {'mean': 8.7,  'max': 18,  'min': 0},
    'Northern Tier BSA':    {'mean': 2.4,  'max': 10,  'min': 0},
    'The Summit Bechtel':   {'mean': 1.2,  'max': 9,   'min': 0},
}

# Monthly averages for the Ranch
monthly_program = {
    1: 38.0, 2: 40.2, 3: 37.2, 4: 41.2,  5: 49.2,
    6: 79.7, 7: 73.5, 8: 37.8, 9: 30.2, 10: 31.0,
    11: 29.7, 12: 27.0
}

# Southeast state comparison
southeast_data = {
    'Florida':        {'the Ranch': 49, 'Florida Sea Base': 44},
    'Georgia':        {'the Ranch': 73, 'Florida Sea Base': 17},
    'South Carolina': {'the Ranch': 91, 'Florida Sea Base': 9},
    'North Carolina': {'the Ranch': 71, 'Florida Sea Base': 16},
    'Alabama':        {'the Ranch': 79, 'Florida Sea Base': 15},
    'Mississippi':    {'the Ranch': 84, 'Florida Sea Base': 16},
}

# Bottom states for the Ranch (recruitment opportunity)
bottom_states = {
    'West Virginia':  21,
    'Florida':        49,
    'Pennsylvania':   68,
    'Maryland':       71,
    'New Hampshire':  71,
    'North Carolina': 71,
    'Ohio':           72,
    'Massachusetts':  72,
    'Virginia':       72,
    'Georgia':        73,
}

# ── BUILD DATAFRAMES ──────────────────────────────────────────
df_annual = pd.DataFrame(annual_trends).T
df_monthly = pd.Series(monthly_program)
df_southeast = pd.DataFrame(southeast_data).T
df_bottom_states = pd.Series(bottom_states)

# Add year and month columns for compatibility with viz cell
search_terms = [
    'the Ranch',
    'Florida Sea Base',
    'Northern Tier BSA',
    'The Summit Bechtel'
]

# ── PRINT SUMMARY ─────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 60)
print("GOOGLE TRENDS SUMMARY STATISTICS")
print("=" * 60)
print("\nOverall averages (relative search interest 0-100):")
for base, stats in trends_summary.items():
    print(f"  {stats['mean']:5.1f} avg  |  {stats['max']:3} max  |  {base}")

print("\nAnnual trends:")
print(df_annual.round(1).to_string())

print("\nMonthly the Ranch search interest:")
month_labels = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun',
                'Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for month, label in zip(range(1,13), month_labels):
    print(f"  {label}: {monthly_program[month]}")

print("\nSoutheast state comparison:")
print(df_southeast.to_string())

print("\nBottom 10 states for the Ranch search interest:")
print(df_bottom_states.sort_values().to_string())

print("""
KEY FINDINGS:
1. BRAND DOMINANCE: the Ranch averages 42.9 vs Florida Sea Base 8.7
   — roughly 5x more search interest nationally

2. DECLINING INTEREST: the Ranch peaked in 2022 (48.3) and has
   declined to 42.3 in 2025 — mirrors participation drop
   Florida Sea Base essentially flat — decline is the Ranch
   specific, not an industry-wide trend

3. SEASONAL WINDOW: June (79.7) and May (49.2) are peak months
   — marketing should concentrate heavily in April through June
   — off-season months Sep-Dec average only 29-31

4. GEOGRAPHIC OPPORTUNITY: West Virginia (21) and Pennsylvania (68)
   have large scouting populations but low the Ranch search interest
   — strong recruitment opportunity in mid-Atlantic region

5. FLORIDA COMPETITION: Only in Florida does another base come close
   — Florida Sea Base scores 44 vs the Ranch 49
   — narrowest margin in any state nationally

6. THE SUMMIT BRAND GAP: The Summit scores 0-5 even in southeastern
   states near its West Virginia location — significant brand
   awareness problem compared to all other bases

7. WESTERN DOMINANCE: Mountain and western states show maximum
   the Ranch search interest — consistent with geographic proximity
   to New Mexico and historical LDS connection in Utah and Idaho
""")

print("✅ Cell 9b complete")

============================================================
GOOGLE TRENDS SUMMARY STATISTICS
============================================================

Overall averages (relative search interest 0-100):
   42.9 avg  |  100 max  |  the Ranch
    8.7 avg  |   18 max  |  Florida Sea Base
    2.4 avg  |   10 max  |  Northern Tier BSA
    1.2 avg  |    9 max  |  The Summit Bechtel

Annual trends:
      the Ranch  Florida Sea Base  Northern Tier BSA  The Summit Bechtel
2020                  39.2               9.8                2.1                 1.5
2021                  43.9               8.2                1.5                 0.3
2022                  48.3              10.8                2.7                 1.3
2023                  43.0               7.7                2.2                 1.3
2024                  40.4               7.4                3.2                 0.9
2025                  42.3               8.2                2.8                 1.5

Monthly the Ranch search interest:
  Jan: 38.0
  Feb: 40.2
  Mar: 37.2
  Apr: 41.2
  May: 49.2
  Jun: 79.7
  Jul: 73.5
  Aug: 37.8
  Sep: 30.2
  Oct: 31.0
  Nov: 29.7
  Dec: 27.0

Southeast state comparison:
                the Ranch  Florida Sea Base
Florida                           49                44
Georgia                           73                17
South Carolina                    91                 9
North Carolina                    71                16
Alabama                           79                15
Mississippi                       84                16

Bottom 10 states for the Ranch search interest:
West Virginia     21
Florida           49
Pennsylvania      68
Maryland          71
New Hampshire     71
North Carolina    71
Ohio              72
Massachusetts     72
Virginia          72
Georgia           73

KEY FINDINGS:
1. BRAND DOMINANCE: the Ranch averages 42.9 vs Florida Sea Base 8.7
   — roughly 5x more search interest nationally

2. DECLINING INTEREST: the Ranch peaked in 2022 (48.3) and has
   declined to 42.3 in 2025 — mirrors participation drop
   Florida Sea Base essentially flat — decline is the Ranch
   specific, not an industry-wide trend

3. SEASONAL WINDOW: June (79.7) and May (49.2) are peak months
   — marketing should concentrate heavily in April through June
   — off-season months Sep-Dec average only 29-31

4. GEOGRAPHIC OPPORTUNITY: West Virginia (21) and Pennsylvania (68)
   have large scouting populations but low the Ranch search interest
   — strong recruitment opportunity in mid-Atlantic region

5. FLORIDA COMPETITION: Only in Florida does another base come close
   — Florida Sea Base scores 44 vs the Ranch 49
   — narrowest margin in any state nationally

6. THE SUMMIT BRAND GAP: The Summit scores 0-5 even in southeastern
   states near its West Virginia location — significant brand
   awareness problem compared to all other bases

7. WESTERN DOMINANCE: Mountain and western states show maximum
   the Ranch search interest — consistent with geographic proximity
   to New Mexico and historical LDS connection in Utah and Idaho

✅ Cell 9b complete

Charting the Google Trends search interest¶

What this does: Draws the search-interest charts from the numbers above — interest over time, by base, by month, and the Ranch vs. Florida Sea Base. Saves google_trends_analysis.png.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL 9c: GOOGLE TRENDS VISUALIZATIONS FROM HARD CODED
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(16, 12))
fig.suptitle('Q6a: Google Search Interest — High Adventure Bases (2020-2025)',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

# ── Chart 1: Annual search interest over time (line chart) ───
for col in search_terms:
    axes[0,0].plot(df_annual.index, df_annual[col], marker='o', label=col, linewidth=2)
axes[0,0].set_title('Annual Search Interest Over Time (US)')
axes[0,0].set_ylabel('Avg Search Interest (0-100)')
axes[0,0].set_xlabel('Year')
axes[0,0].set_xticks(df_annual.index)
axes[0,0].legend(fontsize=8)
axes[0,0].axvline(2022, color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
axes[0,0].annotate('the Ranch peak\n(2022)',
                   xy=(2022, 48.3),
                   xytext=(2022.1, 42),
                   fontsize=8, color='gray',
                   arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='gray', alpha=0.6))

# ── Chart 2: Annual averages bar chart ───────────────────────
x = range(len(df_annual))
width = 0.2
colors_bars = ['steelblue', 'coral', 'mediumseagreen', 'orange']
for i, col in enumerate(search_terms):
    axes[0,1].bar([xi + i*width for xi in x], df_annual[col],
                  width=width, label=col, color=colors_bars[i])
axes[0,1].set_title('Annual Search Interest by Base')
axes[0,1].set_ylabel('Avg Search Interest (0-100)')
axes[0,1].set_xticks([xi + width for xi in x])
axes[0,1].set_xticklabels(df_annual.index)
axes[0,1].legend(fontsize=8)

# ── Chart 3: Monthly seasonality for the Ranch ────────────────
month_labels = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun',
                'Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
monthly_values = [df_monthly[m] for m in range(1,13)]
colors_monthly = ['gold' if m in [5,6,7] else 'steelblue' for m in range(1,13)]
axes[1,0].bar(month_labels, monthly_values, color=colors_monthly)
axes[1,0].set_title('the Ranch Search Interest by Month\n(gold = peak season May-Jul)')
axes[1,0].set_ylabel('Avg Search Interest (0-100)')
axes[1,0].set_xlabel('Month')

# ── Chart 4: Southeast regional comparison ───────────────────
southeast_states = df_southeast.index.tolist()
x_se = range(len(southeast_states))
width_se = 0.35
axes[1,1].bar([xi - width_se/2 for xi in x_se],
              df_southeast['the Ranch'],
              width_se, label='the Ranch', color='steelblue')
axes[1,1].bar([xi + width_se/2 for xi in x_se],
              df_southeast['Florida Sea Base'],
              width_se, label='Florida Sea Base', color='coral')
axes[1,1].set_title('the Ranch vs Florida Sea Base\n(Southeast States)')
axes[1,1].set_ylabel('Relative Search Interest (0-100)')
axes[1,1].set_xticks(list(x_se))
axes[1,1].set_xticklabels(southeast_states, rotation=15, ha='right', fontsize=8)
axes[1,1].legend(fontsize=8)
axes[1,1].annotate('Closest competition\nanywhere nationally',
                   xy=(0, 44),
                   xytext=(0.5, 55),
                   fontsize=8, color='red',
                   arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='red', alpha=0.6))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('google_trends_analysis.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Google Trends visualization saved")

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

✅ Google Trends visualization saved

All the final presentation charts in one place¶

What this does: This is the "polished pictures for the slides" step. It redraws the cleanest version of each main chart and saves them all as image files (q1_response_bias.png, q2_pain_pride.png, q3_return_rate.png, q4_nps_drivers.png, q5_camp_ratings.png, q6a_brand_awareness.png, q6a_barriers.png). These are the images you drop straight into a slide deck.

What to change: If you want different titles on the slide images, edit the titles inside quotes here.

In [ ]:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL 8: All Visualizations
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

#VIZ 1: Q1 Response Bias:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
fig.suptitle('Q1: Survey Response Rate Bias by Crew', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

axes[0].hist(df_response_rates['Adult Responses_rate'].dropna(), bins=20, color='steelblue', edgecolor='white')
axes[0].axvline(df_response_rates['Adult Responses_rate'].mean(), color='red', linestyle='--',
                label=f"Mean: {df_response_rates['Adult Responses_rate'].mean():.1%}")
axes[0].set_title('Adult Response Rate Distribution')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Response Rate')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of Crews')
axes[0].legend()

axes[1].hist(df_response_rates['Youth Responses_rate'].dropna(), bins=20, color='coral', edgecolor='white')
axes[1].axvline(df_response_rates['Youth Responses_rate'].mean(), color='red', linestyle='--',
                label=f"Mean: {df_response_rates['Youth Responses_rate'].mean():.1%}")
axes[1].set_title('Youth Response Rate Distribution')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Response Rate')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Number of Crews')
axes[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q1_response_bias.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Q1 chart saved")

# ── VIZ 2: Q2 Pain Points vs Pride Points ────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
fig.suptitle('Q2: Participant Pain Points vs Pride Points', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

pain_words = [w for w, c in get_top_words(
    df_basecamp['What changes would have improved your experience in base camp?'], n=12)
    if w not in ['nothing', 'good', 'great', 'better']][:8]
pain_counts = []
for word in pain_words:
    text = ' '.join(df_basecamp['What changes would have improved your experience in base camp?'].dropna().astype(str).str.lower())
    pain_counts.append(len(re.findall(r'\b' + word + r'\b', text)))

axes[0].barh(pain_words[::-1], pain_counts[::-1], color='salmon')
axes[0].set_title('Top Pain Point Keywords\n(Base Camp Improvement Suggestions)')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Mention Count')

pride_words = [w for w, c in get_top_words(
    df_basecamp["What was the highlight of your crew's the Ranch Experience?"], n=12)
    if w not in ['highlight', 'experience', 'great', 'camp', 'trek']][:8]
pride_counts = []
for word in pride_words:
    text = ' '.join(df_basecamp["What was the highlight of your crew's the Ranch Experience?"].dropna().astype(str).str.lower())
    pride_counts.append(len(re.findall(r'\b' + word + r'\b', text)))

axes[1].barh(pride_words[::-1], pride_counts[::-1], color='mediumseagreen')
axes[1].set_title('Top Pride Point Keywords\n(Highlight of the Ranch Experience)')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Mention Count')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q2_pain_pride.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Q2 chart saved")

# ── VIZ 3: Q3 Staff Return Rate ──────────────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
fig.suptitle('Q3: Staff Return Rate Analysis', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

label_col_pairs = [
    ('Overall experience',       'How would your rate your experience overall?'),
    ('Work appreciated',         'I feel my work was appreciated.'),
    ('Comfortable being self',   'I felt comfortable and supported being myself as a part of the the Ranch community.'),
    ('Clear expectations',       'My performance expectations were clearly communicated.'),
    ('Good fit for role',        'I feel like I was a good fit for my position.'),
    ('Personal growth',          'I feel I was able to grow as a person through my experience working at the Ranch.'),
    ('Reasonable hours',         'I felt the hours I worked were reasonable for this type of seasonal position.'),
    ('Lasting friendships',      'I created lasting friendships while working at the Ranch.'),
]
corr_pairs = [(label, correlations[col]) for label, col in label_col_pairs]
corr_pairs_sorted = sorted(corr_pairs, key=lambda x: x[1])
labels_sorted, values_sorted = zip(*corr_pairs_sorted)

axes[0].barh(labels_sorted, values_sorted, color='steelblue')
axes[0].set_title('Correlation with Return Likelihood')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Correlation Coefficient')
axes[0].axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
for i, val in enumerate(values_sorted):
    axes[0].text(val + 0.01, i, f'{val:.2f}', va='center', fontsize=9)

dept_return = df_staff.groupby(dept_col)[return_col].mean().sort_values()
dept_return_low = dept_return[dept_return < 3.5]
axes[1].barh(dept_return_low.index, dept_return_low.values, color='salmon')
axes[1].set_title('Departments Below Return Likelihood Threshold\n(threshold = 3.5 / 5)')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Avg Return Likelihood (1-5)')
axes[1].axvline(3.5, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
axes[1].set_xlim(0, 4)
for i, (dept, val) in enumerate(zip(dept_return_low.index, dept_return_low.values)):
    axes[1].text(val + 0.05, i, f'{val:.2f}', va='center', fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q3_return_rate.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Q3 chart saved")

# ── VIZ 4: Q4 NPS & Experience Drivers ───────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
fig.suptitle('Q4: NPS & Experience Drivers', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

scout_counts_list = [scout_counts.get('Promoter',0), scout_counts.get('Passive',0), scout_counts.get('Detractor',0)]
axes[0].pie(scout_counts_list, labels=['Promoters', 'Passives', 'Detractors'],
            colors=['mediumseagreen', 'gold', 'salmon'],
            autopct='%1.1f%%', startangle=90,
            wedgeprops=dict(width=0.5))
axes[0].set_title(f'Scout NPS Breakdown\nNPS Score: {scout_nps}')

driver_labels = list(driver_cols.keys())
driver_corrs = [df_basecamp[col].corr(df_basecamp[overall_col]) for col in driver_cols.values()]
colors_driver = ['steelblue' if c == max(driver_corrs) else 'lightsteelblue' for c in driver_corrs]
axes[1].bar(driver_labels, driver_corrs, color=colors_driver)
axes[1].set_title('What Drives Overall Experience Rating')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Correlation with Overall Rating')
axes[1].set_ylim(0, 0.7)
plt.setp(axes[1].xaxis.get_majorticklabels(), rotation=15, ha='right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q4_nps_drivers.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Q4 chart saved")

# ── VIZ 5: Q5 Individual Camp Ratings ────────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 10))
fig.suptitle('Q5: Backcountry Camp Ratings (2025)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

bottom_camps = camp_pivot.head(15)
axes[0].barh(bottom_camps['camp_name'], bottom_camps['combined_avg'],
             color=['salmon' if v < 3.4 else 'steelblue' for v in bottom_camps['combined_avg']])
axes[0].set_title('Lowest Rated Camps\n(red = below 3.4)')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Combined Staff + Program Avg (1-4 scale)')
axes[0].set_xlim(3.0, 3.9)
axes[0].axvline(3.4, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

top_camps = camp_pivot.tail(15)
axes[1].barh(top_camps['camp_name'], top_camps['combined_avg'], color='mediumseagreen')
axes[1].set_title('Highest Rated Camps')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Combined Staff + Program Avg (1-4 scale)')
axes[1].set_xlim(3.0, 4.0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q5_camp_ratings.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Q5 chart saved")

# ── VIZ 6: Q6a Brand Awareness ───────────────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
fig.suptitle('Q6a: the Ranch Brand Awareness vs Other High Adventure Bases', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

# Familiarity scores — pulled directly from dataframe
base_scores = [df_brand[col + '_score'].mean() for col in familiarity_cols]
colors_bases = ['gold' if name == 'the Ranch' else 'steelblue' for name in base_names]
axes[0].bar(base_names, base_scores, color=colors_bases)
axes[0].set_title('Average Familiarity Score by Base\n(1=not at all, 4=very familiar)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Familiarity Score')
axes[0].set_ylim(0, 4)

# Participation trend — pulled from actual data files
axes[1].plot(years, crews, marker='o', color='steelblue', linewidth=2, markersize=8)
axes[1].fill_between(years, crews, alpha=0.2, color='steelblue')
axes[1].set_title('the Ranch Participation Trend\n(# of Crews per Year)')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Number of Crews')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Year')
axes[1].set_xticks(years)
axes[1].set_ylim(0, 3000)
for x, y in zip(years, crews):
    axes[1].annotate(f'{y:,}', (x, y), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(0, 10), ha='center')

# LDS note as text box since 2019 is before data range
axes[1].text(2021, 2800,
             'Note: LDS Church withdrew\nfrom BSA end of 2019,\npreceding this data range',
             fontsize=7, color='red',
             bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.3', facecolor='white', edgecolor='red', alpha=0.8))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q6a_brand_awareness.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Q6a brand awareness chart saved")

# ── VIZ 7: Q6a Barriers ──────────────────────────────────────
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
barriers = [b for b, c in barrier_counts.most_common()]
counts = [c for b, c in barrier_counts.most_common()]
colors_barrier = ['salmon' if b == 'Cost' else 'steelblue' for b in barriers]
ax.barh(barriers[::-1], counts[::-1], color=colors_barrier[::-1])
ax.set_title('Q6a: Barriers to Attending High Adventure Bases\n(Cost is #1 barrier by wide margin)', fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Number of Mentions')
ax.axvline(50, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q6a_barriers.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Q6a barriers chart saved")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("="*60)
print("\nSaved charts:")
print("  q1_response_bias.png")
print("  q2_pain_pride.png")
print("  q3_return_rate.png")
print("  q4_nps_drivers.png")
print("  q5_camp_ratings.png")
print("  q6a_brand_awareness.png")
print("  q6a_barriers.png")

✅ Q1 chart saved

✅ Q2 chart saved

✅ Q4 chart saved

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

✅ Q6a brand awareness chart saved

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

✅ Q6a barriers chart saved

============================================================
============================================================

Saved charts:
  q1_response_bias.png
  q2_pain_pride.png
  q3_return_rate.png
  q4_nps_drivers.png
  q5_camp_ratings.png
  q6a_brand_awareness.png
  q6a_barriers.png

Understanding the experience score (NPS) — plain-language explainer¶

This write-up explains what the overall recommendation score means and how it is calculated, so anyone reading the slides understands the headline number.

Reflection¶

Graph for Q4 explaination:

  • The three slices:

Green (81.2%) — Promoters: Scouts who gave a 9 or 10 out of 10. These are your enthusiastic fans who will actively recommend the Ranch to other scouts. Yellow (15.7%) — Passives: Scouts who gave a 7 or 8. They had a good experience but aren't enthusiastic enough to actively promote it. They also wouldn't talk negatively about it. Red/Pink (3.1%) — Detractors: Scouts who gave a 1 through 6. These are unhappy participants who might discourage others from attending.

How NPS is calculated from these three groups: NPS = % Promoters - % Detractors = 81.2% - 3.1% = 78.0 I ignored the Passives entirely in the final calculation, since they are just neutral and didn't matter in my eyes. What 78 actually means: NPS scores range from -100 (everyone is a detractor) to +100 (everyone is a promoter). Industry benchmarks generally consider:

Above 0: Good Above 50: Excellent Above 70: World class

the Ranch at 78 is in the world class range, meaning the overwhelming majority of scouts leave wanting to tell other scouts to go. That's a powerful finding for the client — despite complaints about food and trail camp facilities, the overall experience is exceptional.

Where every chart's data comes from¶

So that any reviewer can trace each figure back to its source, here is each chart paired with the exact spreadsheet it came from and how many responses it is based on.

Each charts data source¶

q1_response_bias.png

internal trek-survey response export n = 1,653 crews

q2_pain_pride.png

internal trek-survey results export (base camp & backcountry) n = 4,201 rows (participant responses)

q3_return_rate.png

internal seasonal-staff survey export n = 308 staff respondents

q4_nps_drivers.png

internal trek-survey results export (base camp & backcountry) n = 4,201 rows (participant responses)

q5_camp_ratings.png

internal participant-feedback comparison export (2025 sheet) n = 31 camps with sufficient ratings

q6a_brand_awareness.png (familiarity bar chart)

High adventure knowledge and experience data 2025.csv n = 515 Scoutmaster respondents

q6a_brand_awareness.png (participation line chart)

internal participation-data export (2021-2025 Nationwide Participation files) n = 5 years of crew counts

q6a_barriers.png

High adventure knowledge and experience data 2025.csv n = 515 Scoutmaster respondents

year_over_year.png

internal participant-feedback comparison export (2024 and 2025 sheets)

ranger_subquestions.png

2025 Trek Survey Results Combined - Ranger (as of 11-20-25).xlsx n = 4,127 rows

Outside validation #1 — what scouts say on Reddit¶

What this does: To check whether the survey findings hold up outside the surveys, this step organizes posts from the the program's community forum community by theme (gear, logistics, cost, etc.) and by who's posting. It's a sanity-check that the official survey themes match what people say in the wild. Saves reddit_themes.png.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL X: REDDIT THEMATIC ANALYSIS — the program's community forum
# External validation via manual thematic coding of the program's community forum posts
# Source: the program's community forum feed screenshot, collected May 2026
# No API access — posts manually coded from screenshot sample (n=28)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

print("=" * 60)
print("REDDIT THEMATIC ANALYSIS — the program's community forum (External Validation)")
print("=" * 60)

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

# ── STEP 1: MANUALLY CODED POST DATA ─────────────────────────
# Each post coded from screenshot: title, theme, poster type,
# recency bucket, and which internal survey finding it touches.
# Theme definitions:
#   cost_access      — fees, affordability, financial barriers
#   gear_logistics   — packing, equipment, what to bring
#   trip_planning    — itinerary, routes, program questions
#   staff_experience — staff roles, hiring, living conditions
#   program_quality  — ranger, conservation, campfire, activities

reddit_posts = pd.DataFrame([
    # title                                      theme             poster_type   days_ago  survey_finding
    ("HELP NEEDED FOR RAYADO PACKING LIST",       "gear_logistics", "participant", 6,        "gear_prep"),
    ("Itinerary Guidebook Errors?",               "trip_planning",  "participant", 7,        "program_quality"),
    ("Staff accommodations questions",            "staff_experience","staff",      9,        "staff_satisfaction"),
    ("Base camp staff questions",                 "staff_experience","staff",      12,       "staff_satisfaction"),
    ("12-15 side quests and cantinas",            "trip_planning",  "participant", 13,       "program_quality"),
    ("How does TCT work?",                        "trip_planning",  "participant", 15,       "program_quality"),
    ("drug testing",                              "staff_experience","staff",      16,       "staff_satisfaction"),
    ("Searching for the Ranch Mug Replacement",    "gear_logistics", "alumni",      18,       None),
    ("im gonna be food service staff — high adventure?", "staff_experience","staff", 19,    "staff_satisfaction"),
    ("Staff hiring question",                     "staff_experience","staff",      21,       "staff_satisfaction"),
    ("Tents — freestanding policy?",              "gear_logistics", "participant", 24,       "gear_prep"),
    ("Hiking and rainpants",                      "gear_logistics", "participant", 25,       "gear_prep"),
    ("Water Carrying",                            "gear_logistics", "participant", 25,       "gear_prep"),
    ("Water Reservoirs",                          "trip_planning",  "participant", 28,       "program_quality"),
    ("2026 Staff? Need a ride from Denver on 524","staff_experience","staff",      30,       "staff_satisfaction"),
    ("Personal First Aid",                        "gear_logistics", "participant", 30,       "gear_prep"),
    ("Backpacking Chair",                         "gear_logistics", "participant", 30,       "gear_prep"),
    ("How strict are they on tents being freestanding?", "gear_logistics","participant",30,  "gear_prep"),
    ("How much water for a major peak?",                 "trip_planning",  "participant", 30,       "program_quality"),
    ("Rayado Advice, Packing, Shakedown",         "trip_planning",  "participant", 30,       "program_quality"),
    ("3 Day Trek Ideas",                          "trip_planning",  "staff",       30,       "program_quality"),
    ("Women at the Ranch — packing and training",  "gear_logistics", "participant", 26,       "gear_prep"),
    ("Women — drop your packing list?",           "gear_logistics", "participant", 2,        "gear_prep"),
    ("Need a narrow leather belt option",         "gear_logistics", "participant", 2,        "gear_prep"),
    ("the Ranch vs Sea Base Staff",                "staff_experience","staff",      30,       "staff_satisfaction"),
    ("Making Vehicles Safer at the Ranch",        "trip_planning",  "staff",       30,       None),
    ("a major peak public accessibility?",               "trip_planning",  "alumni",      30,       "program_quality"),
    ("Living in CHQ — roofed housing",            "staff_experience","staff",      1,        "staff_satisfaction"),
], columns=["title", "theme", "poster_type", "days_ago", "survey_finding"])

print(f"\nTotal posts coded: {len(reddit_posts)}")
print(f"Poster breakdown: {reddit_posts['poster_type'].value_counts().to_dict()}")

# ── STEP 2: THEME FREQUENCY ───────────────────────────────────
theme_labels = {
    "gear_logistics":  "Gear & Logistics",
    "trip_planning":   "Trip Planning & Program",
    "staff_experience":"Staff Experience",
    "cost_access":     "Cost & Access",
}
reddit_posts["theme_label"] = reddit_posts["theme"].map(theme_labels)
theme_counts = reddit_posts["theme_label"].value_counts()

print(f"\nTheme frequency:")
for theme, count in theme_counts.items():
    pct = round(count / len(reddit_posts) * 100, 1)
    print(f"  {theme:<30} {count:>2} posts ({pct}%)")

# ── STEP 3: SURVEY ALIGNMENT ──────────────────────────────────
aligned = reddit_posts[reddit_posts["survey_finding"].notna()]
print(f"\nPosts touching a survey finding: {len(aligned)} of {len(reddit_posts)} ({round(len(aligned)/len(reddit_posts)*100,1)}%)")

finding_counts = aligned["survey_finding"].value_counts()
print(f"\nSurvey findings echoed on Reddit:")
finding_labels = {
    "gear_prep":          "Gear / Preparation concerns",
    "program_quality":    "Program quality / trip planning",
    "staff_satisfaction": "Staff experience / working conditions",
}
for finding, count in finding_counts.items():
    print(f"  {finding_labels.get(finding, finding):<40} {count} posts")

# ── STEP 4: VISUALIZATION ─────────────────────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
fig.suptitle("the program's community forum Post Themes — Manual Thematic Coding (n=28)",
             fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")

# Theme distribution
colors = ["#4878CF", "#6ACC65", "#D65F5F", "#B47CC7"]
axes[0].barh(theme_counts.index[::-1], theme_counts.values[::-1], color=colors)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Number of Posts")
axes[0].set_title("Post Volume by Theme")
for i, (val, label) in enumerate(zip(theme_counts.values[::-1], theme_counts.index[::-1])):
    axes[0].text(val + 0.1, i, str(val), va="center", fontsize=10)
axes[0].set_xlim(0, theme_counts.max() + 3)

# Poster type breakdown by theme
poster_theme = reddit_posts.groupby(["theme_label", "poster_type"]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
poster_theme = poster_theme.reindex(theme_counts.index)
poster_theme.plot(kind="bar", ax=axes[1], color=["#B47CC7", "#4878CF", "#6ACC65"],
                  edgecolor="white", width=0.6)
axes[1].set_title("Poster Type by Theme")
axes[1].set_xlabel("")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Number of Posts")
axes[1].tick_params(axis="x", rotation=25)
axes[1].legend(title="Poster Type", fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("reddit_themes.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
print("✅ Reddit theme chart saved")

# ── STEP 5: INTERPRETATION ────────────────────────────────────
print("""
REDDIT THEMATIC ANALYSIS — INTERPRETATION
==========================================

METHOD NOTE:
  the program's community forum does not provide public API access. Posts were manually
  coded from a screenshot sample collected May 2026 (n=28 posts).
  This is a purposive convenience sample and is used for directional
  triangulation only — not for statistical inference.

THEME BREAKDOWN:
  Gear & Logistics is the dominant theme (~39% of posts), followed
  by Trip Planning & Program (~32%) and Staff Experience (~29%).
  Cost & Access generated zero organic posts in this sample window —
  a notable absence given the factors documented in our
  quantitative analysis.

KEY TRIANGULATION FINDINGS:

1. STAFF EXPERIENCE concerns are highly visible on Reddit
   (8 posts, ~29%), consistent with our staff survey finding that
   general staff rate their experience lower than leadership on
   multiple dimensions. Posts cover housing, hiring timelines,
   drug testing, and day-off activities — the same operational
   concerns surfaced in our internal data.

2. PROGRAM QUALITY questions dominate trip planning posts:
   itinerary errors, water availability, ranger shakedown processes,
   and program logistics. This mirrors our survey finding that ranger
   instruction in Base Camp Process and Shakedown are the two
   categories with the largest youth vs. adult rating gaps — scouts
   arriving at the Ranch are actively seeking information on exactly
   these topics before they get there.

3. COST & ACCESS is conspicuously absent from organic Reddit
   discussion despite a 92%+ factor since 2019. This may
   reflect self-selection — users actively planning a trek have
   already cleared the cost barrier. It does not contradict our
   fee/participation findings; it suggests cost is a barrier
   upstream of the Reddit community, before intent is formed.

RECOMMENDATION:
  The Reddit signal is most useful as a leading indicator of
  participant anxiety — what crews are worried about before they
  arrive. Gear prep and shakedown questions cluster in the weeks
  before departure. the Ranch could use this proactively: targeted
  pre-arrival communications on the specific topics Reddit users
  ask about (water carrying, tent policy, packing lists) may reduce
  friction at Base Camp and improve early experience ratings.
""")

print("✅ Reddit thematic analysis complete")

============================================================
REDDIT THEMATIC ANALYSIS — the program's community forum (External Validation)
============================================================

Total posts coded: 28
Poster breakdown: {'participant': 16, 'staff': 10, 'alumni': 2}

Theme frequency:
  Gear & Logistics               11 posts (39.3%)
  Trip Planning & Program         9 posts (32.1%)
  Staff Experience                8 posts (28.6%)

Posts touching a survey finding: 26 of 28 (92.9%)

Survey findings echoed on Reddit:
  Gear / Preparation concerns              10 posts
  Program quality / trip planning          8 posts
  Staff experience / working conditions    8 posts

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

✅ Reddit theme chart saved

REDDIT THEMATIC ANALYSIS — INTERPRETATION
==========================================

METHOD NOTE:
  the program's community forum does not provide public API access. Posts were manually
  coded from a screenshot sample collected May 2026 (n=28 posts).
  This is a purposive convenience sample and is used for directional
  triangulation only — not for statistical inference.

THEME BREAKDOWN:
  Gear & Logistics is the dominant theme (~39% of posts), followed
  by Trip Planning & Program (~32%) and Staff Experience (~29%).
  Cost & Access generated zero organic posts in this sample window —
  a notable absence given the factors documented in our
  quantitative analysis.

KEY TRIANGULATION FINDINGS:

1. STAFF EXPERIENCE concerns are highly visible on Reddit
   (8 posts, ~29%), consistent with our staff survey finding that
   general staff rate their experience lower than leadership on
   multiple dimensions. Posts cover housing, hiring timelines,
   drug testing, and day-off activities — the same operational
   concerns surfaced in our internal data.

2. PROGRAM QUALITY questions dominate trip planning posts:
   itinerary errors, water availability, ranger shakedown processes,
   and program logistics. This mirrors our survey finding that ranger
   instruction in Base Camp Process and Shakedown are the two
   categories with the largest youth vs. adult rating gaps — scouts
   arriving at the Ranch are actively seeking information on exactly
   these topics before they get there.

3. COST & ACCESS is conspicuously absent from organic Reddit
   discussion despite a 92%+ factor since 2019. This may
   reflect self-selection — users actively planning a trek have
   already cleared the cost barrier. It does not contradict our
   fee/participation findings; it suggests cost is a barrier
   upstream of the Reddit community, before intent is formed.

RECOMMENDATION:
  The Reddit signal is most useful as a leading indicator of
  participant anxiety — what crews are worried about before they
  arrive. Gear prep and shakedown questions cluster in the weeks
  before departure. the Ranch could use this proactively: targeted
  pre-arrival communications on the specific topics Reddit users
  ask about (water carrying, tent policy, packing lists) may reduce
  friction at Base Camp and improve early experience ratings.

✅ Reddit thematic analysis complete

Outside validation #2 — national BSA membership vs. the Ranch¶

What this does: Puts national Boy Scouts membership next to the Ranch's crew counts so you can see how much of the Ranch's participation dip is part of a nationwide trend versus something specific to the Ranch. Saves bsa_vs_the ranch.png.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run. (When you add a new year, update the year labels in the titles.)

In [ ]:
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CELL: BSA NATIONAL MEMBERSHIP TREND — two side-by-side charts
#
# SOURCES (all verified):
#   2019 (exact 1,974,635): BSA 2019 Annual Report — ar2019.scouting.org
#   2020 (~1.12M) & 2021 (~762K): AP via AOL News, Jun 2021
#     aol.com/news/boy-scouts-girl-scouts-suffer-141033175-152735470.html
#   2021 confirmed: AP via AOL News, Nov 2023
#     aol.com/retired-businessman-lead-boy-scouts-153146469.html
#   2022 (~1.042M): BSA Scouting Magazine Official Blog, Jan 5 2023
#     blog.scoutingmagazine.org/2023/01/05/1-million-and-growing-bsa-membership-is-on-the-rise/
#   2023 (exact 966,640): BSA 2023 Annual Report — scouting.org/about/annual-report/
#   2024 (exact 978,194): Scouting America 2024 Year-End Infographic
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════


print("=" * 60)
print("BSA MEMBERSHIP vs. THE RANCH — SIDE BY SIDE")
print("=" * 60)

# ── DATA ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
bsa_years   = [2019,    2020,  2021,  2022,   2023,   2024]
bsa_members = [1.975,   1.12,  0.762, 1.042,  0.967,  0.978]  # millions
bsa_exact   = {2019: "1,974,635", 2023: "966,640", 2024: "978,194"}

# the Ranch crew counts — from your participation data
program_years = [2019, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025]
program_crews = [2142, 2222, 1547, 1446, 1359, 1662]

# Key stats
bsa_recovery  = (bsa_members[-1] - bsa_members[2]) / bsa_members[2] * 100   # 2021→2024
crew_trough   = (program_crews[4] - program_crews[1]) / program_crews[1] * 100  # 2021→2024 trough
crew_recovery = (program_crews[-1] - program_crews[4]) / program_crews[4] * 100  # 2024→2025

print(f"BSA  recovery 2021→2024: {bsa_recovery:+.0f}%")
print(f"the Ranch decline 2021→2024: {crew_trough:.0f}%")
print(f"the Ranch partial recovery 2024→2025: {crew_recovery:+.0f}%")

# ── CHART ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COLOR_BSA  = "#2E5596"
COLOR_PRIMARY = "#D65F5F"

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(15, 6.5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("white")

# ── LEFT: BSA Membership ──────────────────────────────────────
ax1.plot(bsa_years, bsa_members, color=COLOR_BSA, linewidth=2.8,
         marker="o", markersize=9, zorder=3)
ax1.fill_between(bsa_years, bsa_members, alpha=0.08, color=COLOR_BSA)

ax1.set_title("BSA Membership\n(Cub Scouts + Scouts BSA)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=12)
ax1.set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=11)
ax1.set_ylabel("Youth Members (millions)", fontsize=11)
ax1.set_xticks(bsa_years)
ax1.set_xlim(2018.5, 2024.5)
ax1.set_ylim(0, 2.4)
ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"{x:.1f}M"))
ax1.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25, linestyle="--")
ax1.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)

# Data labels
bsa_dy = {2019: +0.10, 2020: -0.13, 2021: -0.13, 2022: -0.13, 2023: +0.10, 2024: +0.10}
for yr, val in zip(bsa_years, bsa_members):
    label = bsa_exact[yr] if yr in bsa_exact else f"{val:.3f}M"
    ax1.annotate(label, xy=(yr, val), xytext=(yr, val + bsa_dy[yr]),
                 fontsize=8.5, color=COLOR_BSA, ha="center", fontweight="bold")

# Event annotations
events_bsa = {
    2019: ("LDS withdrawal\n(end of 2019)", -0.28),
    2020: ("COVID-19 +\nbankruptcy", +0.17),
    2022: ("Recovery\n+37%", +0.17),
}
for yr, (label, dy) in events_bsa.items():
    val = bsa_members[bsa_years.index(yr)]
    ax1.annotate(label, xy=(yr, val), xytext=(yr, val + dy),
                 fontsize=8, color="#555555", ha="center",
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#cccccc", lw=0.8),
                 bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.2", fc="white", ec="#dddddd", lw=0.6))

# Recovery shading
ax1.axvspan(2021, 2024.5, alpha=0.04, color="#4CAF50", zorder=0)
ax1.text(2022.75, 2.22, f"Recovery: {bsa_recovery:+.0f}%\n(2021→2024)",
         fontsize=8.5, color="#2E7D32", ha="center", style="italic",
         bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.25", fc="#F1F8F1", ec="#A5D6A7", lw=0.7))

# ── RIGHT: the Ranch Crew Count ────────────────────────────────
bar_colors = [COLOR_PRIMARY if yr < 2025 else "#E8956A" for yr in program_years]
ax2.bar(program_years, program_crews, color=bar_colors,
        alpha=0.75, width=0.55, zorder=3)
ax2.plot(program_years, program_crews, color=COLOR_PRIMARY, linewidth=2.5,
         marker="s", markersize=9, zorder=4)

ax2.set_title("the Ranch Crew Count\n(2019–2025)", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=12)
ax2.set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=11)
ax2.set_ylabel("Number of Crews", fontsize=11)
ax2.set_xticks(program_years)
ax2.set_xlim(2018.5, 2025.5)
ax2.set_ylim(0, 2800)
ax2.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"{int(x):,}"))
ax2.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25, linestyle="--")
ax2.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)

# Data labels
for yr, val in zip(program_years, program_crews):
    ax2.annotate(f"{val:,}", xy=(yr, val), xytext=(yr, val + 55),
                 fontsize=10, color=COLOR_PRIMARY, ha="center", fontweight="bold")

# Decline arrow 2021→2024
ax2.annotate("", xy=(2024, program_crews[4] + 80),
             xytext=(2021, program_crews[1] + 80),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#888888", lw=1.5))
ax2.text(2022.5, program_crews[1] + 220,
         "-39% peak to trough\n(2021 to 2024)",
         fontsize=9, color="#B71C1C", ha="center", fontweight="bold",
         bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.3", fc="#FFF5F5", ec="#FFCDD2", lw=0.8))

# 2025 partial recovery annotation
ax2.annotate(f"Partial recovery\n{crew_recovery:+.0f}% (2024→2025)",
             xy=(2025, program_crews[5]),
             xytext=(2024.3, program_crews[5] + 320),
             fontsize=8.2, color="#E65100", ha="center",
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#E65100", lw=1.0),
             bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.25", fc="#FFF8F0", ec="#FFCC80", lw=0.7))

# ── SHARED SUBTITLE ───────────────────────────────────────────
fig.suptitle(
    "BSA membership recovered +28% from its 2021 trough — the Ranch declined −39% over the same period",
    fontsize=11, style="italic", color="#333333", y=1.01
)

# ── SOURCE NOTE ───────────────────────────────────────────────
fig.text(
    0.01, -0.07,
    "BSA SOURCES (Cub Scouts + Scouts BSA combined):  "
    "2019 (exact): ar2019.scouting.org  |  "
    "2020–2021: aol.com/news/boy-scouts-girl-scouts-suffer-141033175-152735470.html  |  "
    "2021 (confirmed): aol.com/retired-businessman-lead-boy-scouts-153146469.html\n"
    "2022: blog.scoutingmagazine.org/2023/01/05/1-million-and-growing-bsa-membership-is-on-the-rise/  |  "
    "2023 (exact): scouting.org/about/annual-report/  |  "
    "2024 (exact): Scouting America 2024 Year-End Infographic\n"
    "the Ranch crew counts: internal participant-feedback comparison export — '2023 & Prior' sheet (2019-2023); 2024 & 2025 sheets (total crews from 'Crews: X of Y' row).",
    fontsize=7.2, color="#666666", va="top"
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("bsa_vs_program.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

# ── INTERPRETATION ─────────────────────────────────────────────
print(f"""
INTERPRETATION
==============
THE RANCH CREW COUNT (from your data):
  2019: 2,142  |  2021: 2,222  |  2022: 1,547  |  2023: 1,446  |  2024: 1,359  |  2025: 1,662

  Peak→trough decline (2021→2024): {crew_trough:.0f}%
  Partial recovery (2024→2025): {crew_recovery:+.0f}%

BSA (Cub Scouts + Scouts BSA):
  2021 trough: ~762K  →  2024: ~978K  ({bsa_recovery:+.0f}% recovery)

KEY DIVERGENCE:
  BSA's national addressable pool recovered +{bsa_recovery:.0f}% from 2021→2024.
  the Ranch crews fell {abs(crew_trough):.0f}% over the same period.

  The 2025 partial the Ranch recovery (+{crew_recovery:.0f}%) is worth noting —
  it suggests demand exists but may be constrained. Consistent with
  the fee/participation narrative: not a permanent exit from scouting,
  but a cost barrier that suppresses attendance year to year.

  Correlation ≠ causation. This is one additional directional signal.
""")

print("✅ Chart saved: bsa_vs_program.png")

============================================================
BSA MEMBERSHIP vs. THE RANCH — SIDE BY SIDE
============================================================
BSA  recovery 2021→2024: +28%
the Ranch decline 2021→2024: -39%
the Ranch partial recovery 2024→2025: +22%

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

INTERPRETATION
==============
THE RANCH CREW COUNT (from your data):
  2019: 2,142  |  2021: 2,222  |  2022: 1,547  |  2023: 1,446  |  2024: 1,359  |  2025: 1,662

  Peak→trough decline (2021→2024): -39%
  Partial recovery (2024→2025): +22%

BSA (Cub Scouts + Scouts BSA):
  2021 trough: ~762K  →  2024: ~978K  (+28% recovery)

KEY DIVERGENCE:
  BSA's national addressable pool recovered +28% from 2021→2024.
  the Ranch crews fell 39% over the same period.

  The 2025 partial the Ranch recovery (+22%) is worth noting —
  it suggests demand exists but may be constrained. Consistent with
  the fee/participation narrative: not a permanent exit from scouting,
  but a cost barrier that suppresses attendance year to year.

  Correlation ≠ causation. This is one additional directional signal.

✅ Chart saved: bsa_vs_the ranch.png

Appendix — return rates for every department¶

What this does: A complete version of the staff return-rate chart showing all departments, kept in the appendix so the main section stays focused. Saves q3_all_departments_appendix.png.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ── APPENDIX: Q3 All Departments Return Rate ─────────────────
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))

dept_return_all = df_staff.groupby(dept_col)[return_col].mean().sort_values()

# Green = above threshold (pride/high retention), red = below
colors = ['mediumseagreen' if val >= 3.5 else 'salmon'
          for val in dept_return_all.values]

ax.barh(dept_return_all.index, dept_return_all.values, color=colors)
ax.set_title('Return Likelihood by Department — All Departments\n(threshold = 3.5 / 5)',
             fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Avg Return Likelihood (1-5)')
ax.axvline(3.5, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, label='3.5 threshold')
ax.set_xlim(0, 5)

for i, (dept, val) in enumerate(zip(dept_return_all.index, dept_return_all.values)):
    ax.text(val + 0.05, i, f'{val:.2f}', va='center', fontsize=9)

ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('q3_all_departments_appendix.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("✅ Appendix all-departments chart saved")

[chart removed for de-identification — see notes]

✅ Appendix all-departments chart saved

Appendix — checking whether council-level data is available¶

What this does: A quick behind-the-scenes check of whether council-by-council data exists to support the "look at participation by region" recommendation. This is a data-availability check, not a chart.

What to change: Nothing.

Appendix — which councils declined the most¶

What this does: Lists the councils with the biggest participation drops between 2021 and 2024 — useful for the "target outreach to declining councils" recommendation.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

Appendix — data source totals (the numbers cited on the slides)¶

What this does: Prints the response counts (the "n =" numbers) for each data source so every figure on the slides can be traced back to exactly how many responses it's based on.

What to change: Nothing required to re-run.

In [ ]:
# ── DATA SOURCE TOTALS — matches numbers cited on slides ──────
print("=" * 60)
print("DATA SOURCE TOTALS")
print("=" * 60)

# Trek Survey — Base Camp & Backcountry (Slide 4, n = 4,201)
print(f"\nTrek Survey — Base Camp & Backcountry:")
print(f"  Rows: {len(df_basecamp):,}")

# Trek Survey — Ranger (Data sources slide, n = 4,127)
print(f"\nTrek Survey — Ranger:")
print(f"  Rows: {len(df_ranger):,}")

# Trek Survey — Conservation (Data sources slide)
print(f"\nTrek Survey — Conservation:")
print(f"  Rows: {len(df_conservation):,}")

# Staff Survey (Slide 5, n = 308)
print(f"\nSeasonal Staff End of Season Survey:")
print(f"  Rows: {len(df_staff):,}")

# Response Rate Tracker (Data sources slide, n = 1,653 crews)
print(f"\nTrek Survey Response Rate Tracker:")
print(f"  Crews: {len(df_response_rates):,}")

# Brand Awareness — Scoutmasters (Slide 9, n = 515)
print(f"\nHigh Adventure Brand Awareness Survey:")
print(f"  Scoutmaster respondents: {len(df_brand):,}")

# Participation Files (2021-2025)
print(f"\nNationwide Participation Files (internal participation-data export):")
for fname, df in sorted(participation_frames.items()):
    year = fname[:4]
    total_crews = df['# of Crews'].sum()
    print(f"  {year}: {len(df):,} rows, {int(total_crews):,} total crews")

# Reddit (Slide 8, n = 28 posts)
print(f"\nthe program's community forum Manual Thematic Coding:")
print(f"  Posts coded: 28 (May 2026, manual count)")

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("SLIDE CITATION VERIFICATION")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"  Slide 1 — Base Camp & Backcountry n = {len(df_basecamp):,}  (deck says 4,201)")
print(f"  Slide 1 — Ranger n = {len(df_ranger):,}  (deck says 4,127)")
print(f"  Slide 1 — Staff n = {len(df_staff):,}  (deck says 308)")
print(f"  Slide 1 — Response tracker n = {len(df_response_rates):,}  (deck says 1,653)")
print(f"  Slide 1 — Scoutmasters n = {len(df_brand):,}  (deck says 515)")
print(f"  Slide 5 — Staff responses n = {len(df_staff):,}  (deck says 308)")
print(f"  Slide 8 — the program's community forum n = 28  (deck says 28, manual count)")

============================================================
DATA SOURCE TOTALS
============================================================

Trek Survey — Base Camp & Backcountry:
  Rows: 4,201

Trek Survey — Ranger:
  Rows: 4,127

Trek Survey — Conservation:
  Rows: 4,201

Seasonal Staff End of Season Survey:
  Rows: 308

Trek Survey Response Rate Tracker:
  Crews: 1,653

High Adventure Brand Awareness Survey:
  Scoutmaster respondents: 515

Nationwide Participation Files (internal participation-data export):
  2021: 1,813 rows, 2,407 total crews
  2022: 1,255 rows, 1,712 total crews
  2023: 1,266 rows, 1,613 total crews
  2024: 1,100 rows, 1,395 total crews
  2025: 1,287 rows, 1,693 total crews

the program's community forum Manual Thematic Coding:
  Posts coded: 28 (May 2026, manual count)

============================================================
SLIDE CITATION VERIFICATION
============================================================
  Slide 1 — Base Camp & Backcountry n = 4,201  (deck says 4,201)
  Slide 1 — Ranger n = 4,127  (deck says 4,127)
  Slide 1 — Staff n = 308  (deck says 308)
  Slide 1 — Response tracker n = 1,653  (deck says 1,653)
  Slide 1 — Scoutmasters n = 515  (deck says 515)
  Slide 5 — Staff responses n = 308  (deck says 308)
  Slide 8 — the program's community forum n = 28  (deck says 28, manual count)

Wrapping up¶

That's the whole analysis. To recap what just ran:

  • The notebook loaded every the Ranch survey and spreadsheet, cleaned them, and turned ratings into numbers it could work with.
  • It answered each research question in turn — who responds to surveys, what people praise and complain about, whether staff return, how strong the overall experience score is, how individual camps rate, and how well-known the Ranch is among troops.
  • It zoomed out to the big picture: the long-run participation trend, the relationship between rising fees and falling participation, and outside checks from Reddit and national BSA membership.
  • It saved clean chart images ready to drop into a slide deck, and listed exactly where every number came from.

To run it again next year: put the new files in a Drive folder, update the data_dir line and the file names in the Load and clean all the files step, fix any chart titles or year labels that should change, and click Runtime → Run all. That's it.

If anything breaks, paste the red error text into Gemini or Google, or reach out to the student analyst who built this. Good luck, and thanks for keeping the the Ranch story going.

Appendix: Use of AI in This Project¶

Overview¶

This codebook was built with the help of an AI tool (Claude), used as a support tool throughout the project — for planning, debugging code, organizing the analysis, checking the internal consistency of numbers, and rewriting the notebook into a non-technical format for the client.

At no point did the AI have access to the actual the Ranch data. Since this project uses real, confidential data from a real client, the underlying datasets were never uploaded or shared with the AI. The most I ever provided was a column header or the structure of a file so the tool could understand what I was working with. All of the real code was written and tested by me, against the real data, on my own machine.

Planning and project setup¶

Early on, I used AI to turn my ideas from the project brief into a working roadmap. It helped me by breaking the research questions into phases, ordering them so they built on each other, and suggesting analysis approaches for each (for example, text and sentiment analysis for the open-ended survey responses, and regression for the staff return-rate question). The strategy and the decisions were mine; AI helped me structure the plan and figure out where to start.

Writing and debugging code¶

When I hit an error or wasn't sure how to write a particular step, I described what I was trying to do and asked for help. I shared only column names or file structure, never the data itself. I wrote, ran, and tested all of the real code myself. AI helped me get unstuck, suggested visualization code (such as the participation-trend charts), and explained fixes that I then had to understand well enough to apply correctly.

Organizing the analysis and presentation¶

I used AI to review my draft notebook and flag what was missing before I built the presentation. For example, identifying that the NPS score was carrying too much of the argument, helping crystallize my findings into a clear set of recommendations, and tightening the central "ask." I made the final calls on what to keep, cut, or reframe.

Manual work the AI did not do¶

Several of the most important parts were done entirely by me, by hand:

  • Sourcing and verifying every real number. The BSA membership figures, the trek fee schedule, and the year-over-year crew counts all came directly from original documents and spreadsheets that I located and confirmed myself.
  • Coding the Reddit data manually. Because there was no API access to the program's community forum, I read and thematically coded the posts by hand (manual count).
  • Confirming all outputs. Since the AI never saw the data, it could not run the notebook or verify a single result. Every output was produced and checked by me.

Checking numbers and external facts¶

I used AI to check the internal consistency of the numbers across my codebook and slides (the percentages, ratios, and NPS math all tie out), and to look up publicly available external facts, such as the national BSA youth membership figures, so I could verify them against the public record. This caught a few real mistakes such as a wrong membership figure on one slide and some leftover editing notes, which I then corrected. The AI did not interpret any of the the Ranch numbers; it only helped me sanity-check figures I had already confirmed from the source documents.

Converting the notebook into a non-technical codebook¶

Because the client is not technical, I asked AI to help rewrite my finished notebook into a clean codebook that the Ranch staff could follow and re-run themselves each year, matching the easy-to-follow style of a notebook from my Optimization class project. It kept all of my code, calculations, and charts exactly as I wrote them and added the explanatory wrapper around them: a front-matter section (what the notebook is, a workflow overview, step-by-step directions for re-running it, and tips for first-time users), plus a short plain-English explainer before each code step describing what it does and exactly what to change each year. It then helped export the notebook to HTML. I also used it to clean up grammar and wording in my write-ups.

What I learned¶

AI was genuinely useful for getting unstuck on code and for documentation, but it could only suggest, not implement. I still had to understand each fix well enough to apply it correctly to data the tool never saw, and I had to review the final output myself to make sure every chart title, year label, and explanation matched what the code actually does. Writing the plain-language documentation also forced me to understand my own code well enough to explain every step simply. The biggest takeaway was that documentation written for the next person is just as important as the analysis itself: a project no one else can re-run loses most of its value the moment I leave.
